The blueprint-doctor flags every literal `REF` as a malformed reference.
197	- **Do NOT interleave math delimiters.** Pick `\( … \)` for a formula and close it with `\)` — never produce `$ … \( … \) … $` or `X\(, hence \)Y` inversions; the doctor's `math-delim` lint flags every such site and the rendered output shreds mid-formula.
198	- **Do NOT paste bare label ids into prose.** "Thm.~th:main" is not a reference — write `\cref{...}` for project declarations, or the source's human-readable number ("Thm.~4.8") when quoting external material.
199	- **Do NOT use undefined macros.** Every `\foo` your prose needs must exist in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex` or be `\providecommand`'d at the top of your chapter (chapter-local is fine; report a "needs macro" note for shared ones).
200	- **Do NOT touch protected blocks.** Your invocation prompt's "Protected by the mathematician" section lists `archon-protected.yaml` rules. A `statement`-protected label's declaration block (statement + `\label`/`\lean`/`\uses`) is frozen — you may still write its `\begin{proof}`; an `all`-protected label is entirely off-limits. You may freely `\uses{}` protected labels from other blocks. If your directive conflicts with a protection rule, stop and report the conflict instead of editing.
201	- **Do NOT expand scope.** Stick to what the directive listed under "Required content".
202	- **Do NOT fabricate citations.** Never write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from memory. If you don't have a local `references/<file>.md` containing the source text, dispatch a retriever and wait. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical is the discipline check — if you cannot truthfully point to the file you read, the citation block is not allowed in the chapter.
203	- **Do NOT translate or restate the verbatim quote.** `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` contain the source's original language, original notation, every word as-is. Project-notation rewrites belong in the rendered prose body, not in the verbatim comment.
204	
205	## Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)
206	
207	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:
208	
209	- makes the reliance explicit and readable (a mathematician sees exactly which Mathlib result the route stands on);
210	- gives `leandag` a real node, so the `\uses{}` resolves (no broken/unknown ref) and the node's effort is **done** rather than ∞;
211	- keeps it clear how much the route leans on Mathlib vs. on original work.
212	
213	Write the anchor like any statement block, with three differences:
214	
215	```latex
216	\begin{lemma}[Hensel's lemma]
217	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}
218	  \lean{Mathlib.RingTheory.Henselian.foo}   % the REAL Mathlib declaration
219	  \mathlibok
220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
221	  Statement of the result in the project's notation.
222	\end{lemma}
223	```
224	
225	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
226	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
227	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
228	
229	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. A wrong or invented `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop will treat a non-existent result as available and skip proving a real gap. So:
230	
231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.
234	
235	## Reading references
236	
237	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
238	
239	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
240	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
241	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
242	
243	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
244	
245	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
246	
247	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
248	
249	Conditions for dispatch:
250	
251	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
252	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
253	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
254	
255	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
256	
257	```
258	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
259	  --name reference-retriever \
260	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
261	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
262	  --write-domain 'references/**'
263	```
264	
265	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
266	
267	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
268	
269	## Workflow
270	
271	1. Read your directive completely.
272	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references. Track which local files you actually opened — you will need their paths for the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
273	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
274	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow. For each block that derives from an external source, identify the specific local `references/<file>.md` containing the verbatim statement (and proof, if applicable). If no local file covers the statement, list it as "retrieval needed" for the next step.
275	5. **For every "retrieval needed" item, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and **wait** for it to return. THEN open and read the newly-written `references/<slug>.md` before drafting the citing block. Do not draft citation blocks against pending or imagined sources.
276	6. Make the edits. For each citation block: copy `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` content character-by-character from the local reference file — original language, original notation, every word. Do not transcribe from a window of the file you "remember reading"; have the file open and copy.
277	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`). Spot-check that every `% SOURCE:` line has a non-empty `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical and that the named file exists.
278	8. **Verify dependencies with `leandag`** (see the section below): confirm your chapter introduced no broken `\uses{}` and left none of your blocks unintentionally isolated. Fix the edges and re-check.
279	9. Write your report. List under "References consulted" every local file you opened in step 2 and step 5.
280	
281	## Verifying dependencies with leandag
282	
283	`leandag` parses the real Lean ↔ blueprint dependency DAG, so use it to check your `\uses{}` instead of trusting your memory of the graph. It is read-only on the blueprint (it never edits your chapter); run it after you make edits:
284	
285	```
286	leandag build --json                       # report: unknown_uses, unmatched_lean, isolated count
287	leandag query --isolated --chapter <c>     # isolated nodes in your chapter (no edges in or out)
288	leandag show isolated                       # all isolated nodes, project-wide
289	```
290	
291	`leandag` is on the same PATH as `archon`; it is the one tool you use to query the DAG.
292	
293	What to do with the output, scoped to **your chapter**:
294	
295	- **`unknown_uses` naming a label in your chapter** → you wrote a `\uses{}` pointing at a label that doesn't exist. Fix the label, or — if the target genuinely belongs in your chapter and the directive calls for it — add the missing declaration. If the missing label lives in another chapter, leave a correct `\uses{}` to it and note it under "Notes for Plan Agent" (you don't edit that chapter).
296	- **A block you wrote shows up as isolated** → it has no `\uses{}` out and nothing uses it. This is almost always a *missing edge*: add the `\uses{}` that ties it to the declarations its statement/proof actually depends on (and check whether a sibling result should `\uses{}` it). Only treat an isolated block as removable when your directive explicitly authorized removing it.
297	- **Removal** (directive-authorized only): delete the orphaned block cleanly and record it in your report. Never remove the goal or a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
298	
299	Keep `leandag` edits inside your assigned chapter — fixing a dependency that lives in another chapter is a "Notes for Plan Agent" item, not a cross-chapter edit.
300

10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.

15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.

18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.

23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).

26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)

83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.

91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
159

1	\chapter{{\v C}ech computation of higher direct images $R^i f_*$ (unconditional)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechHigherDirectImage.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
5	% quasi-coherent sheaves and \S Quasi-coherence of higher direct images
6	% (Lemmas \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial},
7	% \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero},
8	% \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent},
9	% \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}) (read from
10	% references/stacks-coherent.tex).
11	
12	\section{Setup and motivation}
13	
14	This chapter constructs the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
15	\(i \geq 1\) \emph{without appealing to injective resolutions in the category of
16	sheaves of modules}. The companion development of
17	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage} defines \(R^i f_*\) as a right
18	derived functor, which in a formalisation requires the ambient category of
19	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to have enough injectives; that property is not currently
20	available for sheaves of modules over a sheaf of rings whose value category varies
21	over the site. The {\v C}ech approach developed here sidesteps the issue entirely:
22	it computes \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as the cohomology of an explicit complex built
23	from the pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the finite intersections of an affine
24	open cover, and so produces an \emph{unconditional} construction of \(R^i f_*\) for
25	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) and separated quasi-compact \(f\).
26	
27	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
28	separated (so that all finite intersections of an affine open cover of \(X\) are
29	again affine), and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. We
30	write \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) for the category of quasi-coherent
31	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\)
32	is recorded by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
33	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
34	\[
35	  \begin{array}{ccc}
36	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
37	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
38	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
39	  \end{array}
40	\]
41	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
42	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
43	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
44	
45	\section{The {\v C}ech nerve and {\v C}ech complex of an affine cover}
46	
47	\begin{definition}
48	
49	\leanok
50	[{\v C}ech nerve of an affine open cover]
51	  \label{def:cech_nerve}
52	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechNerve}
53	  \uses{def:cover_cech_nerve, lem:push_pull_id, lem:push_pull_comp}
54	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
55	  % quasi-coherent sheaves, opening paragraph and Lemma
56	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}
57	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L36--67).
58	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
59	  % Let $U \subset X$ be an affine open.
60	  % Recall that a {\it standard open covering} of $U$ is a covering
61	  % of the form $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$
62	  % where $f_1, \ldots, f_n \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)$ generate
63	  % the unit ideal, see
64	  % Schemes, Definition \ref{schemes-definition-standard-covering}."
65	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
66	  quasi-coherent sheaves.}
67	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
68	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite open cover of \(X\) by
69	  affine opens, with index set \(I\). For a finite nonempty tuple
70	  \(i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I\) write
71	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = U_{i_0} \cap \cdots \cap U_{i_p}\) for the corresponding
72	  intersection. The \emph{{\v C}ech nerve} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the augmented
73	  \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) whose object in
74	  cosimplicial degree \(p\) is the direct image, along the open immersions
75	  \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\), of the restriction
76	  of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (or of any chosen quasi-coherent sheaf) to the
77	  \((p+1)\)-fold intersections,
78	  \[
79	    [p] \;\longmapsto\;
80	      \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
81	        (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_* \,\bigl(\,\cdot\,|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr),
82	  \]
83	  with cofaces given by the restriction maps that omit one index and
84	  codegeneracies by the maps that repeat one index; the augmentation in degree
85	  \(-1\) is the object on all of \(X\). The variance is essential: the geometric
86	  nerve of the cover (the iterated fibre powers of \(\coprod_i U_i\) over \(X\),
87	  introduced in \S\ref{sec:cech_three_part} below) is a \emph{simplicial} object
88	  in \emph{schemes}, but applying the covariant direct-image functor turns it into
89	  a \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); consequently the
90	  associated alternating-coface complex is a \emph{cochain} complex (differentials
91	  raising degree), not a chain complex. When \(X\) is separated each intersection
92	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is itself affine. The local affine model is the
93	  \emph{standard open covering} \(U = \bigcup_{i=1}^n D(f_i)\) of an affine open
94	  \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) by basic opens, where \(f_1, \ldots, f_n \in A\)
95	  generate the unit ideal; on such a cover the intersections are again basic opens
96	  \(D(f_{i_0} \cdots f_{i_p})\).
97	\end{definition}
98	
99	\begin{definition}
100	
101	\leanok
102	[{\v C}ech complex of a quasi-coherent sheaf]
103	  \label{def:cech_complex}
104	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechComplex}
105	  \uses{def:cech_nerve, def:relative_cech_complex_of_nerve}
106	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
107	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (proof)
108	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L59--77).
109	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Clearly the {\v C}ech complex
110	  % $\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$
111	  % is identified with the complex
112	  % $$
113	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
114	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
115	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
116	  % \ldots
117	  % $$
118	  % We are asked to show that the extended complex
119	  % \begin{equation}
120	  % \label{equation-extended}
121	  % 0 \to
122	  % M \to
123	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
124	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
125	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
126	  % \ldots
127	  % \end{equation}
128	  % (whose truncation we have studied in
129	  % Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover-module}) is exact."
130	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
131	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (standard-cover {\v C}ech
132	  vanishing).}
133	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and let
134	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover with
135	  all intersections \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) affine (e.g.\ \(X\) separated). The
136	  \emph{{\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
137	  is the cochain complex obtained by applying the global-sections functor to the
138	  {\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} evaluated at \(\mathcal{F}\);
139	  in degree \(p\) it is
140	  \[
141	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
142	      = \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
143	        \mathcal{F}\bigl(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
144	  \]
145	  with differential \(d : \check{\mathcal{C}}^p \to \check{\mathcal{C}}^{p+1}\) the
146	  alternating sum \((d s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} = \sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\,
147	  s_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}}\) of the
148	  restriction maps. Over an affine \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) with
149	  \(\mathcal{F}|_U = \widetilde{M}\) and a standard cover by the
150	  \(D(f_i)\), this is the complex
151	  \(\prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}} \to \cdots\) of
152	  localisations. The \emph{relative {\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet
153	  (\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) for \(f : X \to S\) is the analogous complex in
154	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) obtained by applying \(f_*\) over each finite
155	  intersection instead of global sections; its terms are
156	  \(\prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} (f|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})_*
157	  \bigl(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr)\), and it is a complex in
158	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) because each \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is affine and the
159	  pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf along a quasi-compact quasi-separated
160	  morphism is quasi-coherent.
161	\end{definition}
162	
163	\subsection{The three-part construction of the relative {\v C}ech complex}
164	\label{sec:cech_three_part}
165	
166	It is convenient to factor the construction of
167	Definitions~\ref{def:cech_nerve}--\ref{def:cech_complex} into three independent
168	pieces. Two of them are purely formal and unconditional; the entire mathematical
169	content that is not yet available off the shelf is isolated in the middle piece.
170	
171	\paragraph{(1) Geometric backbone — the augmented {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow.}
172	Package the affine cover as a single morphism
173	\(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) (the canonical map out of the
174	coproduct induced by the inclusions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\)). The augmented
175	{\v C}ech nerve of this arrow is an augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
176	\(X\): its object in simplicial degree \(p\) is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
177	\[
178	  \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
179	    \;=\;
180	    \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
181	      U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
182	\]
183	with the augmentation the cover map to \(X\). Because the \(U_i\) are open
184	subschemes of \(X\), each fibre product \(U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p}\)
185	is canonically the intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\). This backbone exists
186	\emph{unconditionally}: it uses only that the category of schemes has coproducts
187	and finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the {\v C}ech nerve of an
188	arrow). It carries no sheaf-theoretic data yet — it is the simplicial diagram of
189	spaces over which the construction is indexed.
190	
191	\paragraph{(2) Push--pull functor — lifting the backbone to modules.}
192	The geometric backbone is turned into a cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module by
193	the \emph{relative-direct-image functor on the over-category}
194	\[
195	  G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \longrightarrow \operatorname{QCoh}(X),
196	  \qquad
197	  (Y \xrightarrow{\,p\,} X) \;\longmapsto\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
198	\]
199	sending an \(X\)-scheme \(p : Y \to X\) to the pushforward along \(p\) of the
200	pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\). Composing \(G\) with the (opposite of the) simplicial
201	backbone of (1) produces exactly the augmented cosimplicial object of
202	Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: on the degree-\(p\) piece
203	\(p : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\) one has
204	\(p_* p^*\mathcal{F} = (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\),
205	and a morphism of \(X\)-schemes \(h : (Y_1, p_1) \to (Y_2, p_2)\) (so
206	\(h \circ p_2 = p_1\)\,, reading \(p\) as the structure map) induces the
207	restriction comparison \(G(p_2) \to G(p_1)\) via the unit/counit of the
208	pull-push adjunction together with the over-triangle identifying the two
209	structure maps. This functor \(G\) is the \emph{one non-formal step} in the whole
210	construction. Its functoriality --- that \(G\) respects identities and composition
211	of morphisms over \(X\) --- is precisely a statement about the coherence of the
212	comparison isomorphisms \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) and
213	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\) for pushforward and pullback of sheaves of
214	modules, together with their compatibility with the adjunction units. This
215	functoriality is \emph{not} coupled to the project's tensor--pullback substrate;
216	it consumes only Mathlib's pseudofunctor packaging of pullback and pushforward.
217	Concretely, pullback and pushforward of sheaves of modules are organised as a
218	pseudofunctor \(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to
219	\mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\) (sending a morphism to the adjoint pair
220	\(p^* \dashv p_*\)), and the comparison isomorphisms
221	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\), \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) are its structure
222	\(2\)-cells. The functoriality of \(G\) needs exactly three facts about this
223	packaging: the mate identity expressing the conjugate of the inverse pullback
224	comparison as the pushforward comparison (the lemma
225	\texttt{conjugateEquiv\_pullbackComp\_inv}), its identity normalisation
226	\texttt{conjugateEquiv\_pullbackId\_hom}, and the pseudofunctor unitor and pentagon
227	(associativity) coherences \texttt{pseudofunctor\_left\_unitality},
228	\texttt{pseudofunctor\_right\_unitality}, \texttt{pseudofunctor\_associativity}.
229	All of these are already available off the shelf, so the functoriality of the
230	relative-direct-image functor is a \emph{consumer of Mathlib's coherence},
231	independent of the project-local sheafification/pullback machinery of the
232	tensor--pullback substrate. The laws are nonetheless not formal trivialities: each
233	is a multi-step mate calculation that transports the comparison isomorphisms along
234	the over-triangle \(g \circ p_2 = p_1\) of structure maps and past the adjunction
235	unit, gluing them across two \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions; the obstacle is the
236	bookkeeping of those transports, not a missing geometric ingredient.
237	
238	\paragraph{(3) Coherence-free plumbing — from the nerve to the cochain complex.}
239	The passage from the augmented cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module of (1)--(2)
240	to the relative {\v C}ech complex in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) is again purely
241	formal and uses \emph{no} pushforward/pullback coherence. One forgets the
242	augmentation, pushes the cosimplicial object forward along \(f : X \to S\) (a
243	covariant operation applied degreewise), and takes the alternating-coface-map
244	cochain complex of the resulting cosimplicial object of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules.
245	This step relies only on the preadditivity of \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\): the
246	alternating sum \(\sum_{j} (-1)^j d^j\) of the cofaces is defined in any
247	preadditive category and squares to zero by the cosimplicial identities. Thus the
248	relative {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
249	of Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} is genuinely \emph{defined} in terms of the
250	{\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: once the nerve is constructed,
251	the complex follows with no further input. In summary, of the three pieces only
252	the push--pull functor (2) requires content beyond formal category theory, and
253	that content is the pushforward/pullback pseudofunctor coherence supplied by
254	Mathlib (the comparison \(2\)-cells of the pseudofunctor
255	\(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to \mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\)
256	and their unitor/pentagon identities), not any project-local ingredient.
257	
258	\paragraph{Formal bricks of the three-part construction.}
259	The geometric backbone (1), the push--pull functor (2), and the plumbing (3) are
260	recorded as the following declarations. The backbone and plumbing are
261	unconditional; the push--pull object and morphism maps are likewise unconditional
262	bricks, and the functor laws assembling them are the lone coherence statement.
263	
264	\begin{definition}\leanok
265	[Arrow of an affine cover]
266	  \label{def:cover_arrow}
267	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverArrow}
268	  Let \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be an affine open cover. Its
269	  \emph{cover arrow} is the single morphism
270	  \(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) obtained as the universal map out of
271	  the coproduct induced by the open immersions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\). Packaging
272	  the cover as one arrow lets the generic {\v C}ech-nerve-of-an-arrow machinery
273	  apply.
274	\end{definition}
275	
276	\begin{definition}\leanok
277	[Augmented {\v C}ech nerve of a cover]
278	  \label{def:cover_cech_nerve}
279	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerve}
280	  \uses{def:cover_arrow}
281	  The augmented {\v C}ech nerve of the cover arrow of
282	  Definition~\ref{def:cover_arrow}: the augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
283	  \(X\) whose degree-\(p\) object is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
284	  \[
285	    \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
286	      = \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
287	        U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
288	  \]
289	  with augmentation the cover map to \(X\). It exists unconditionally because the
290	  category of schemes has all finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the
291	  {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow). This is the geometric backbone (1).
292	\end{definition}
293	
294	\begin{definition}\leanok
295	[Push--pull object map]
296	  \label{def:push_pull_obj}
297	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullObj}
298	  The object map of the push--pull functor \(G\): to an \(X\)-scheme
299	  \(p : Y \to X\) it assigns
300	  \[
301	    G(Y, p) \;=\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
302	  \]
303	  the pushforward along \(p\) of the pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\).
304	\end{definition}
305	
306	\begin{definition}\leanok
307	[Push--pull comparison map]
308	  \label{def:push_pull_map}
309	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap}
310	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj}
311	  The morphism map of \(G\). A morphism \(g : (Y_2, p_2) \to (Y_1, p_1)\) of
312	  \(X\)-schemes has underlying map \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) satisfying the
313	  over-triangle \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\); the contravariant functor produces the
314	  restriction comparison \(G(Y_1, p_1) \to G(Y_2, p_2)\) as the five-step composite
315	  \[
316	    p_{1*}\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
317	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{1*}(\eta)\,}
318	    p_{1*}\,\bar g_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
319	      \xrightarrow{\,\sim\,}
320	    (p_1 \circ \bar g)_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
321	      \;=\;
322	    p_{2*}\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
323	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{2*}(\sim)\,}
324	    p_{2*}\,p_2^*\mathcal{F},
325	  \]
326	  where \(\eta\) is the unit of the adjunction \(\bar g^* \dashv \bar g_*\), the
327	  second arrow is the pushforward comparison \(p_{1*}\bar g_* \cong (p_1 \bar g)_*\),
328	  the equality is the substitution \(p_2 = p_1 \circ \bar g\) of the over-triangle,
329	  and the last arrow applies \(p_{2*}\) to the pullback comparison
330	  \(\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (p_1 \bar g)^* = p_2^*\). The two over-triangle
331	  substitutions are realised as coercions along \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\).

145	\begin{lemma}
146	\label{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero}
147	\begin{slogan}
148	Serre vanishing: Higher cohomology vanishes on affine schemes
149	for quasi-coherent modules.
150	\end{slogan}
151	Let $X$ be a scheme.
152	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
153	For any affine open $U \subset X$ we have
154	$H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ for all $p > 0$.
155	\end{lemma}
156	
157	\begin{proof}
158	We are going to apply
159	Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-cech-vanish-basis}.
160	As our basis $\mathcal{B}$ for the topology of $X$ we are going to use
161	the affine opens of $X$.
162	As our set $\text{Cov}$ of open coverings we are going to use the standard
163	open coverings of affine opens of $X$.
164	Next we check that conditions (1), (2) and (3) of
165	Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-cech-vanish-basis}
166	hold. Note that the intersection of standard opens in an affine is
167	another standard open. Hence property (1) holds.
168	The coverings form a cofinal system of open coverings of any element
169	of $\mathcal{B}$, see
170	Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-standard-open}.
171	Hence (2) holds.
172	Finally, condition (3) of the lemma follows from
173	Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}.
174	\end{proof}
175	
176	\noindent
177	Here is a relative version of the vanishing of cohomology of quasi-coherent
178	sheaves on affines.
179	
180	\begin{lemma}
181	\label{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing}
182	Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
183	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
184	If $f$ is affine then $R^if_*\mathcal{F} = 0$ for all $i > 0$.
185	\end{lemma}
186	
187	\begin{proof}
188	According to
189	Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}
190	the sheaf
191	$R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
192	$V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$.
193	By assumption, whenever $V$ is affine we have that $f^{-1}(V)$ is
194	affine, see Morphisms, Definition \ref{morphisms-definition-affine}.
195	By Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero} we conclude that
196	$H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}) = 0$
197	whenever $V$ is affine. Since $S$ has a basis consisting of affine
198	opens we win.
199	\end{proof}
200	
201	\begin{lemma}
202	\label{lemma-relative-affine-cohomology}
203	Let $f : X \to S$ be an affine morphism of schemes.
204	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
205	Then $H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) = H^i(S, f_*\mathcal{F})$ for all $i \geq 0$.
206	\end{lemma}
207	
208	\begin{proof}
209	Follows from Lemma \ref{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing}
210	and the Leray spectral sequence. See
211	Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-apply-Leray}.
212	\end{proof}
213	
214	\noindent
215	The following two lemmas explain when {\v C}ech cohomology
216	can be used to compute cohomology of quasi-coherent modules.
217	
218	\begin{lemma}
219	\label{lemma-affine-diagonal}
220	Let $X$ be a scheme. The following are equivalent
221	\begin{enumerate}
222	\item $X$ has affine diagonal $\Delta : X \to X \times X$,
223	\item for $U, V \subset X$ affine open, the intersection
224	$U \cap V$ is affine, and
225	\item there exists an open covering $\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i$
226	such that $U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}$ is affine open for all $p \ge 0$ and all
227	$i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I$.
228	\end{enumerate}
229	In particular this holds if $X$ is separated.

740	
741	\begin{lemma}
742	\label{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images}
743	Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
744	Assume that $f$ is quasi-separated and quasi-compact.
745	\begin{enumerate}
746	\item For any quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module $\mathcal{F}$ the
747	higher direct images $R^pf_*\mathcal{F}$ are quasi-coherent on $S$.
748	\item If $S$ is quasi-compact, there exists an integer $n = n(X, S, f)$
749	such that $R^pf_*\mathcal{F} = 0$ for all $p \geq n$ and any
750	quasi-coherent sheaf $\mathcal{F}$ on $X$.
751	\item In fact, if $S$ is quasi-compact we can find $n = n(X, S, f)$
752	such that for every
753	morphism of schemes $S' \to S$ we have $R^p(f')_*\mathcal{F}' = 0$
754	for $p \geq n$ and any quasi-coherent sheaf $\mathcal{F}'$
755	on $X'$. Here $f' : X' = S' \times_S X \to S'$ is the base change of $f$.
756	\end{enumerate}
757	\end{lemma}
758	
759	\begin{proof}
760	We first prove (1). Note that under the hypotheses of the lemma the sheaf
761	$R^0f_*\mathcal{F} = f_*\mathcal{F}$ is quasi-coherent by
762	Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-push-forward-quasi-coherent}.
763	Using
764	Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-localize-higher-direct-images}
765	we see that forming higher direct images commutes with restriction
766	to open subschemes. Since being quasi-coherent is local on $S$ we
767	reduce to the case discussed in the next paragraph.
768	
769	\medskip\noindent
770	Proof of (1) in case $S$ is affine. We will use the induction principle.
771	Since $f$ quasi-compact and quasi-separated we see that $X$
772	is quasi-compact and quasi-separated. For $U \subset X$
773	quasi-compact open and $a = f|_U$ we let $P(U)$ be the property that
774	$R^pa_*\mathcal{F}$ is quasi-coherent on $S$ for all quasi-coherent modules
775	$\mathcal{F}$ on $U$ and all $p \geq 0$. Since $P(X)$ is (1), it suffices
776	the prove conditions (1) and (2) of Lemma \ref{lemma-induction-principle} hold.
777	If $U$ is affine, then $P(U)$ holds because $R^pa_*\mathcal{F} = 0$ for
778	$p \geq 1$ (by  Lemma \ref{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing} and
779	Morphisms, Lemma \ref{morphisms-lemma-morphism-affines-affine})
780	and we've already observed the result holds for $p = 0$ in the first
781	paragraph. Next, let $U \subset X$ be a quasi-compact open, $V \subset X$
782	an affine open, and assume $P(U)$, $P(V)$, $P(U \cap V)$ hold.
783	Let $a = f|_U$, $b = f|_V$, $c = f|_{U \cap V}$, and $g = f|_{U \cup V}$.
784	Then for any quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_{U \cup V}$-module $\mathcal{F}$
785	we have the relative Mayer-Vietoris sequence
786	$$
787	0 \to
788	g_*\mathcal{F} \to
789	a_*(\mathcal{F}|_U) \oplus b_*(\mathcal{F}|_V) \to
790	c_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U \cap V}) \to
791	R^1g_*\mathcal{F} \to \ldots
792	$$
793	see Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-relative-mayer-vietoris}.
794	By $P(U)$, $P(V)$, $P(U \cap V)$ we see that
795	$R^pa_*(\mathcal{F}|_U)$, $R^pb_*(\mathcal{F}|_V)$ and
796	$R^pc_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U \cap V})$ are all quasi-coherent.
797	Using the results on quasi-coherent sheaves in
798	Schemes, Section \ref{schemes-section-quasi-coherent}
799	this implies that each of the sheaves
800	$R^pg_*\mathcal{F}$ is quasi-coherent since it sits in the middle of a short
801	exact sequence with a cokernel of a map between quasi-coherent sheaves
802	on the left and a kernel of a map between quasi-coherent sheaves on the right.
803	Whence $P(U \cup V)$ and the proof of (1) is complete.
804	
805	\medskip\noindent
806	Next, we prove (3) and a fortiori (2). Choose a finite affine open
807	covering $S = \bigcup_{j = 1, \ldots m} S_j$. For each $j$ choose
808	a finite affine open covering
809	$f^{-1}(S_j) = \bigcup_{i = 1, \ldots t_j} U_{ji} $.
810	Let
811	$$
812	d_j = \max\nolimits_{I \subset \{1, \ldots, t_j\}}
813	\left(|I| + t(\bigcap\nolimits_{i \in I} U_{ji})\right)
814	$$
815	be the integer found in
816	Lemma \ref{lemma-vanishing-nr-affines-quasi-separated}.
817	We claim that $n(X, S, f) = \max d_j$ works.
818	
819	\medskip\noindent
820	Namely, let $S' \to S$ be a morphism of schemes and let
821	$\mathcal{F}'$ be a quasi-coherent sheaf on $X' = S' \times_S X$.
822	We want to show that $R^pf'_*\mathcal{F}' = 0$ for $p \geq n(X, S, f)$.
823	Since this question is local on $S'$ we may assume that $S'$ is affine
824	and maps into $S_j$ for some $j$. Then $X' = S' \times_{S_j} f^{-1}(S_j)$
825	is covered by the open affines $S' \times_{S_j} U_{ji}$, $i = 1, \ldots t_j$
826	and the intersections
827	$$
828	\bigcap\nolimits_{i \in I} S' \times_{S_j} U_{ji} =
829	S' \times_{S_j} \bigcap\nolimits_{i \in I} U_{ji}
830	$$
831	are covered by the same number of affines as before the base change.
832	Applying
833	Lemma \ref{lemma-vanishing-nr-affines-quasi-separated}
834	we get $H^p(X', \mathcal{F}') = 0$. By the first part of the proof
835	we already know that each $R^qf'_*\mathcal{F}'$ is quasi-coherent
836	hence has vanishing higher cohomology groups on our affine scheme $S'$,
837	thus we see that $H^0(S', R^pf'_*\mathcal{F}') = H^p(X', \mathcal{F}') = 0$
838	by Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-apply-Leray}.
839	Since $R^pf'_*\mathcal{F}'$ is quasi-coherent
840	we conclude that $R^pf'_*\mathcal{F}' = 0$.
841	\end{proof}
842	
843	\begin{lemma}
844	\label{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}
845	Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
846	Assume that $f$ is quasi-separated and quasi-compact.
847	Assume $S$ is affine.
848	For any quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module $\mathcal{F}$
849	we have
850	$$
851	H^q(X, \mathcal{F}) = H^0(S, R^qf_*\mathcal{F})
852	$$
853	for all $q \in \mathbf{Z}$.
854	\end{lemma}
855	
856	\begin{proof}
857	Consider the Leray spectral sequence $E_2^{p, q} = H^p(S, R^qf_*\mathcal{F})$
858	converging to $H^{p + q}(X, \mathcal{F})$, see
859	Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-Leray}.
860	By Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images}
861	we see that the sheaves $R^qf_*\mathcal{F}$ are quasi-coherent.
862	By Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero}
863	we see that $E_2^{p, q} = 0$ when $p > 0$.
864	Hence the spectral sequence degenerates at $E_2$ and we win.
865	See also
866	Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-apply-Leray} (2)
867	for the general principle.
868	\end{proof}

36	Let $X$ be a scheme.
37	Let $U \subset X$ be an affine open.
38	Recall that a {\it standard open covering} of $U$ is a covering
39	of the form $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$
40	where $f_1, \ldots, f_n \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)$ generate
41	the unit ideal, see
42	Schemes, Definition \ref{schemes-definition-standard-covering}.
43	
44	\begin{lemma}
45	\label{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}
46	Let $X$ be a scheme.
47	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
48	Let $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$ be a standard
49	open covering of an affine open of $X$.
50	Then $\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ for
51	all $p > 0$.
52	\end{lemma}
53	
54	\begin{proof}
55	Write $U = \Spec(A)$ for some ring $A$.
56	In other words, $f_1, \ldots, f_n$ are elements of $A$
57	which generate the unit ideal of $A$.
58	Write $\mathcal{F}|_U = \widetilde{M}$ for some $A$-module $M$.
59	Clearly the {\v C}ech complex
60	$\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$
61	is identified with the complex
62	$$
63	\prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
64	\prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
65	\prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
66	\ldots
67	$$
68	We are asked to show that the extended complex
69	\begin{equation}
70	\label{equation-extended}
71	0 \to
72	M \to
73	\prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
74	\prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
75	\prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
76	\ldots
77	\end{equation}
78	(whose truncation we have studied in
79	Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover-module}) is exact.
80	It suffices to show that (\ref{equation-extended})
81	is exact after localizing at a prime $\mathfrak p$, see
82	Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-characterize-zero-local}.
83	In fact we will show that the extended complex localized
84	at $\mathfrak p$ is homotopic to zero.
85	
86	\medskip\noindent
87	There exists an index $i$ such that $f_i \not \in \mathfrak p$.
88	Choose and fix such an element $i_{\text{fix}}$. Note that
89	$M_{f_{i_{\text{fix}}}, \mathfrak p} = M_{\mathfrak p}$. Similarly
90	for a localization at a product $f_{i_0} \ldots f_{i_p}$ and $\mathfrak p$
91	we can drop any $f_{i_j}$ for which $i_j = i_{\text{fix}}$.
92	Let us define a homotopy
93	$$
94	h :
95	\prod\nolimits_{i_0 \ldots i_{p + 1}}
96	M_{f_{i_0} \ldots f_{i_{p + 1}}, \mathfrak p}
97	\longrightarrow
98	\prod\nolimits_{i_0 \ldots i_p}
99	M_{f_{i_0} \ldots f_{i_p}, \mathfrak p}
100	$$
101	by the rule
102	$$
103	h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{i_{\text{fix}} i_0 \ldots i_p}
104	$$
105	(This is ``dual'' to the homotopy in the proof of
106	Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-homology-complex}.)
107	In other words, $h : \prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}, \mathfrak p} \to M_\mathfrak p$
108	is projection onto the factor
109	$M_{f_{i_{\text{fix}}}, \mathfrak p} = M_{\mathfrak p}$ and in general
110	the map $h$ equal projection onto the factors
111	$M_{f_{i_{\text{fix}}} f_{i_1} \ldots f_{i_{p + 1}}, \mathfrak p}
112	= M_{f_{i_1} \ldots f_{i_{p + 1}}, \mathfrak p}$. We compute
113	\begin{align*}
114	(dh + hd)(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p}
115	& =
116	\sum\nolimits_{j = 0}^p
117	(-1)^j
118	h(s)_{i_0 \ldots \hat i_j \ldots i_p}
119	+
120	d(s)_{i_{\text{fix}} i_0 \ldots i_p}\\
121	& =
122	\sum\nolimits_{j = 0}^p
123	(-1)^j
124	s_{i_{\text{fix}} i_0 \ldots \hat i_j \ldots i_p}
125	+
126	s_{i_0 \ldots i_p}
127	+
128	\sum\nolimits_{j = 0}^p
129	(-1)^{j + 1}
130	s_{i_{\text{fix}} i_0 \ldots \hat i_j \ldots i_p} \\
131	& =
132	s_{i_0 \ldots i_p}
133	\end{align*}
134	This proves the identity map is homotopic to zero as desired.
135	\end{proof}
136	
137	\noindent
138	The following lemma says in particular that for any affine scheme
139	$X$ and any quasi-coherent sheaf $\mathcal{F}$ on $X$ we have
140	$$
141	H^p(X, \mathcal{F}) = 0
142	$$
143	for all $p > 0$.
144	
145	\begin{lemma}
146	\label{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero}
147	\begin{slogan}
148	Serre vanishing: Higher cohomology vanishes on affine schemes
149	for quasi-coherent modules.
150	\end{slogan}
151	Let $X$ be a scheme.
152	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
153	For any affine open $U \subset X$ we have
154	$H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ for all $p > 0$.
155	\end{lemma}
156	
157	\begin{proof}
158	We are going to apply
159	Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-cech-vanish-basis}.
160	As our basis $\mathcal{B}$ for the topology of $X$ we are going to use
161	the affine opens of $X$.
162	As our set $\text{Cov}$ of open coverings we are going to use the standard
163	open coverings of affine opens of $X$.
164	Next we check that conditions (1), (2) and (3) of
165	Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-cech-vanish-basis}
166	hold. Note that the intersection of standard opens in an affine is
167	another standard open. Hence property (1) holds.
168	The coverings form a cofinal system of open coverings of any element
169	of $\mathcal{B}$, see
170	Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-standard-open}.

[{\v C}ech acyclicity on affines]
  \label{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechAcyclic.affine}
  \uses{def:cech_complex}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Tag 02KG (affine vanishing);
  % Lemma \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (standard-cover
  % {\v C}ech vanishing) and Lemma
  % \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero} (Serre vanishing on affines)
  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L44--52 and L145--155).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
  % Let $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$ be a standard
  % open covering of an affine open of $X$.
  % Then $\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ for
  % all $p > 0$."
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Serre vanishing: Higher cohomology vanishes on affine schemes
  % for quasi-coherent modules.
  % Let $X$ be a scheme.
  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
  % For any affine open $U \subset X$ we have
  % $H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ for all $p > 0$."
  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (standard-cover {\v C}ech
  vanishing) and Stacks, Tag 02KG
  (\texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero}, Serre vanishing on
  affines).}
  Let \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) be an affine scheme, \(\mathcal{F}\) a
  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module, and
  \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i=1}^n D(f_i)\) a standard open cover (so the
  \(f_i \in A\) generate the unit ideal). Then the {\v C}ech complex
  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\) has vanishing cohomology
  in all positive degrees:
  \[
    \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0 \qquad \text{for all } p > 0,
  \]
  and consequently \(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for all \(p > 0\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}

\leanok
[Standard-cover {\v C}ech-complex vanishing on affines]
  \label{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechAcyclic.affine}
  \uses{def:cech_complex}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (standard-cover
  % {\v C}ech vanishing) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L44--52).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
  % Let $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$ be a standard
  % open covering of an affine open of $X$.
  % Then $\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ for
  % all $p > 0$."
  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (standard-cover {\v C}ech
  vanishing).}
  Let \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) be an affine scheme, \(\mathcal{F}\) a
  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module, and
  \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i=1}^n D(f_i)\) a standard open cover (so the
  \(f_i \in A\) generate the unit ideal). Then the {\v C}ech complex
  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
  Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} has vanishing cohomology in all positive
  degrees:
  \[
    \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0 \qquad \text{for all } p > 0.
  \]
  Equivalently, the extended complex
  \(0 \to M \to \prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}}
  \to \cdots\) of localisations is exact. This is the standard-cover {\v C}ech
  vanishing only; the sheaf-cohomology Serre vanishing
  \(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) is the separate
  Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing} below, obtained from this complex
  vanishing by the basis-comparison Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis}.
\end{lemma}

  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Serre vanishing: Higher cohomology vanishes on affine schemes
  % for quasi-coherent modules.
  % Let $X$ be a scheme.
  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
  % For any affine open $U \subset X$ we have
  % $H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ for all $p > 0$."
  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Tag 02KG
  (\texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero}, Serre vanishing on
  affines).}
  Let \(U\) be an affine scheme and \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
  \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module. Then the sheaf cohomology of \(\mathcal{F}\) vanishes in
  all positive degrees:
  \[
    H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0 \qquad \text{for all } p > 0.
  \]
\end{lemma}

  \texttt{cohomology-lemma-cech-vanish-basis} (as applied in the proof of

  set of admissible open covers be given, subject to: (1) \(\mathcal{B}\) is closed

  of \(B\) are cofinal among all open covers of \(B\); and (3) for every admissible
  cover \(\mathcal{U}\) of every \(B \in \mathcal{B}\), every quasi-coherent

  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0\). Then for every \(B \in \mathcal{B}\)
  and every \(p\) the canonical comparison map is an isomorphism
  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \cong H^p(B, \mathcal{F})\); in particular,

  the finite intersections of \(\mathcal{U}\) (these lie in \(\mathcal{B}\) by (1)),

\end{proof}

\section{The {\v C}ech complex computes $R^i f_*$}

The comparison is proved by the \emph{acyclic-resolution} (Cartan--Leray) route. The
augmented {\v C}ech complex is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) whose terms are
acyclic for \(f_*\); the abstract homological-algebra lemma
\(\cref{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived}\) then identifies the derived
functor with the cohomology of the complex obtained by applying \(f_*\). The two
geometric inputs are isolated in the following sub-lemmas.

\begin{lemma}
[The augmented {\v C}ech complex is a resolution]
  \label{lem:cech_augmented_resolution}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechAugmented_exact}
  \uses{def:cech_nerve, lem:cech_acyclic_affine}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (proof: exactness of the
  % extended {\v C}ech complex) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L68--82).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We are asked to show that the extended complex
  % \begin{equation}
  % \label{equation-extended}
  % 0 \to
  % M \to
  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
  % \ldots
  % \end{equation}
  % (whose truncation we have studied in
  % Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover-module}) is exact.
  % It suffices to show that (\ref{equation-extended})
  % is exact after localizing at a prime $\mathfrak p$, see
  % Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-characterize-zero-local}."
  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (proof).}
  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and let
  \(\mathfrak{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover with all
  intersections affine. Form the augmented {\v C}ech complex on \(X\),
  \[
    0 \;\to\; \mathcal{F} \;\to\; \mathcal{C}^0 \;\to\; \mathcal{C}^1 \;\to\;
    \mathcal{C}^2 \;\to\; \cdots,
    \qquad
    \mathcal{C}^p \;=\; \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
      (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*\bigl(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr),
  \]
  the degree-\(p\) term being the degree-\(p\) object of the {\v C}ech nerve of
  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}, with augmentation the canonical map
  \(\mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{C}^0\). This augmented complex is exact; that is, the
  {\v C}ech nerve is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:cech_nerve, lem:cech_acyclic_affine}
  Exactness of a complex of sheaves is a local question on \(X\), so it may be
  checked on the stalks, equivalently over each affine open of a basis. Restricting
  to an affine open \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) on which \(\mathcal{F}|_U =
  \widetilde{M}\) and over which the cover restricts to a standard cover \(U =
  \bigcup D(f_i)\), the augmented {\v C}ech complex becomes the extended complex of
  localisations
  \(0 \to M \to \prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}} \to
  \cdots\). Its exactness is exactly the standard-cover vanishing of
  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}, witnessed by the prime-local contracting
  homotopy \(h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{i_{\mathrm{fix}} i_0 \ldots i_p}\). As this
  holds over every affine of the basis, the augmented complex is exact on \(X\).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[Each {\v C}ech term is $f_*$-acyclic]
  \label{lem:cech_term_pushforward_acyclic}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechTerm_pushforward_acyclic}
  \uses{lem:affine_serre_vanishing, def:cech_nerve}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
  % \texttt{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing} (relative vanishing of higher direct
  % images for affine morphisms) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
  % L180--199).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
  % If $f$ is affine then $R^if_*\mathcal{F} = 0$ for all $i > 0$."
  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
  \texttt{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing} (relative affine vanishing).}
  Let \(f : X \to S\) be separated and quasi-compact, \(\mathcal{F}\) a
  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(\mathfrak{U}\) a finite affine open
  cover of \(X\) with all intersections affine. Then for every \(p \geq 0\) the
  degree-\(p\) {\v C}ech term \(\mathcal{C}^p =
  \prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\)
  is right \((\,\cdot\,)_*\)-acyclic for the pushforward \(f_*\):
  \[
    (f_*).\mathrm{rightDerived}\,k\,(\mathcal{C}^p) = 0
      \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1.
  \]
\end{lemma}

% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "According to
% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}
% the sheaf
% $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
% $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$.
% By assumption, whenever $V$ is affine we have that $f^{-1}(V)$ is
% affine, see Morphisms, Definition \ref{morphisms-definition-affine}.
% By Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero} we conclude that
% $H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}) = 0$
% whenever $V$ is affine. Since $S$ has a basis consisting of affine
% opens we win."
\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:affine_serre_vanishing, def:cech_nerve}
  A product of acyclic objects is acyclic, so it suffices to treat a single factor
  \((j_s)_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_s})\), where \(s = (i_0, \ldots, i_p)\), \(U_s =
  U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is affine, and \(j_s : U_s \hookrightarrow X\) is the open
  immersion. Acyclicity for \(f_*\) is a local question on \(S\): the derived
  pushforward \(R^k f_*\, \mathcal{G}\) is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
  \(V \mapsto H^k(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{G}|_{f^{-1}(V)})\), so it vanishes for
  \(k \geq 1\) iff that cohomology vanishes for all affine \(V \subseteq S\).
  Restricting to such a \(V = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), the base change \(f_V :
  f^{-1}(V) \to V\) is again separated and quasi-compact over the separated base
  \(V\), hence \(f^{-1}(V)\) is separated and the affine \(U_s\) meets it in the
  affine open \(U_s \cap f^{-1}(V)\).

  Now decompose the structure map \(g_s := f \circ j_s : U_s \to S\). First,
  \(j_s\) is the inclusion of an affine open into the separated scheme \(X\), hence
  an affine morphism, so the higher direct images \(R^q (j_s)_*\) vanish for
  \(q \geq 1\); consequently the Grothendieck composition spectral sequence for
  \(f \circ j_s\) degenerates and \(R^k f_*\bigl((j_s)_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_s})\bigr) =
  R^k (g_s)_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_s})\). Second, working locally over an affine
  \(V \subseteq S\) as above, \(g_s\) restricts to a morphism of affine schemes
  \(U_s \cap f^{-1}(V) \to V\), which is an affine morphism; by the relative affine
  vanishing \(R^k (g_s)_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_s}) = 0\) for \(k \geq 1\). That relative
  vanishing is itself a consequence of Serre vanishing
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing}): \(R^k(g_s)_*\mathcal{G}\) is the
  sheafification of \(V \mapsto H^k(g_s^{-1}(V), \mathcal{G})\), and for affine
  \(V\) the preimage \(g_s^{-1}(V)\) is affine, on which higher cohomology of a
  quasi-coherent sheaf vanishes. Combining, \(R^k f_*(\mathcal{C}^p) = 0\) for all
  \(k \geq 1\).
\end{proof}

  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is affine, and \(f\) restricted to it is an affine
  morphism; by the affine acyclicity of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} the
  higher direct images of \(\mathcal{F}\) over each such intersection vanish, so the
  terms of the relative {\v C}ech complex carry no hidden higher cohomology.

  Consider the {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequence of the cover
  \(\mathfrak{U}\), with \(E_2\)-page
  \(E_2^{p,q} = \check{H}^p(\mathfrak{U}, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}))\)
  converging to \(H^{p+q}(X, \mathcal{F})\). Since all intersections are affine,
  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} forces the presheaf cohomology
  \(\underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F})\) to vanish on the intersections for \(q > 0\), so
  the spectral sequence collapses to its row \(q = 0\): the {\v C}ech cohomology of
  \(\mathcal{F}\) for the cover \(\mathfrak{U}\) computes the sheaf cohomology,
  \(\check{H}^p(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F})\) for all \(p\),
  and the same identification holds relatively, i.e.\ the cohomology sheaves of
  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) compute the cohomology
  sheaves of \(Rf_*\mathcal{F}\).

  It remains to identify these cohomology sheaves with \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\). Over
  the affine base \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), the Leray spectral sequence
  \(E_2^{p,q} = H^p(S, R^q f_*\mathcal{F}) \Rightarrow H^{p+q}(X, \mathcal{F})\)
  has \(R^q f_*\mathcal{F}\) quasi-coherent, whence \(H^p(S, R^q f_*\mathcal{F}) = 0\)
  for \(p > 0\) by Serre vanishing (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}). The Leray
  sequence therefore degenerates at \(E_2\), giving
  \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) = H^0(S, R^i f_*\mathcal{F})\). Combining with the previous
  paragraph, \(H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\bigr)
  \cong R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules for all \(i \geq 0\).

  \emph{This proof depends on the following currently-absent Mathlib
  infrastructure: the {\v C}ech-to-derived-functor spectral sequence and the Leray
  spectral sequence for the category of sheaves of modules on a scheme, neither of
  which is presently available there; the argument uses both to collapse onto the
  comparison isomorphism.}
\end{proof}

  The source proves the statement by exhibiting it, in view of Serre vanishing on
  affines, as a special case of the {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral-sequence
  criterion. We organise the same fact as the standard \emph{acyclic-resolution}
  (Cartan--Leray) argument, which needs no spectral sequence beyond the single
  abstract homological-algebra lemma \(\cref{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived}\).

  Apply the abstract lemma with the right-derivable additive functor \(G = f_*\)
  (the pushforward \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X) \to \operatorname{QCoh}(S)\)), the object
  \(A = \mathcal{F}\), and the resolution \(J^\bullet = \mathcal{C}^\bullet\) given by
  the {\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}. Its two hypotheses are
  exactly the geometric sub-lemmas above:
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item By Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_augmented_resolution} the augmented {\v C}ech
      complex \(0 \to \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{C}^0 \to \mathcal{C}^1 \to \cdots\) is
      exact, i.e.\ \(\mathcal{C}^\bullet\) is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) in
      \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\).
    \item By Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_term_pushforward_acyclic} each term
      \(\mathcal{C}^p\) is right \(f_*\)-acyclic:
      \((f_*).\mathrm{rightDerived}\,k\,(\mathcal{C}^p) = 0\) for \(k \geq 1\).
  \end{enumerate}
  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived} then gives, for every \(i\), an
  isomorphism between the \(i\)-th derived functor evaluated at \(\mathcal{F}\) and
  the \(i\)-th cohomology of the complex obtained by applying \(f_*\) to the
  resolution,
  \[
    (f_*).\mathrm{rightDerived}\,i\,(\mathcal{F})
      \;\cong\;
    H^i\bigl(f_*\,\mathcal{C}^\bullet\bigr).
  \]
  By construction (Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex}) the complex \(f_*\,
  \mathcal{C}^\bullet\) is precisely the relative {\v C}ech complex
  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\), so its \(i\)-th
  cohomology is \(H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})
  \bigr)\); and \((f_*).\mathrm{rightDerived}\,i\,(\mathcal{F})\) is the
  derived-functor higher direct image \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) of
  Definition~\ref{def:higher_direct_image}. This yields the isomorphism
  \(H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\bigr) \cong
  R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) asserted by the lemma; taking it in the existence
  (\(\operatorname{Nonempty}\)) form discards the choice of resolution, as required.
  Over an affine base \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), passing to global sections
  recovers \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) = \check{H}^i(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^0(S,
  R^i f_*\mathcal{F})\).

  isolated in two places: the abstract acyclic-resolution lemma
  \(\cref{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived}\) (a pure homological-algebra

  Lemmas~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} and~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing}. No
  {\v C}ech-to-derived-functor spectral sequence and no Leray spectral sequence for
  sheaves of modules on a scheme are needed.}
\end{proof}
